name: Chinese (Simplified)

formality.description: Chinese does not have a T-V pronoun distinction — 你 (nǐ) is standard for 'you' and 您 (nín) is the respectful form but rarely used in software UI. Formality in Chinese is expressed through vocabulary choice, sentence structure complexity, and the use of four-character idioms (成语 chéngyǔ). Software UI uses clear, modern Mandarin with no classical Chinese phrasing.

gender.inclusiveGuidance: Spoken Chinese pronouns are gender-neutral (tā). Written Chinese distinguishes 他 (he), 她 (she), 它 (it), but gender-neutral 他 is conventionally used for unknown gender. In UI text, prefer restructuring to avoid third-person pronouns entirely.

professional.label: 专业 (Professional)
professional.description: Standard for software UI, websites, and business communication in mainland China.
professional.prompt: Professional Simplified Chinese (简体中文). Clear, modern Mandarin appropriate for software interfaces and business communication. Use 你 (not 您) for second person — 您 is overly deferential for most UI contexts. Avoid classical Chinese phrasing and four-character idioms unless they're commonly understood. Keep sentences concise.

formal.label: 正式 (Formal)
formal.description: For government, enterprise, legal, or official contexts where elevated language is expected.
formal.prompt: Formal Simplified Chinese. Use 您 for respectful second-person address. Elevated register appropriate for government portals, legal text, and enterprise communication. Precise vocabulary, longer sentence structures acceptable.

casual.label: 轻松 (Casual)
casual.description: For consumer apps, social media, gaming, and youth-oriented products.
casual.prompt: Casual Simplified Chinese. Conversational, friendly tone for consumer products. Short sentences, modern internet-influenced vocabulary acceptable. Emoji-adjacent language style.

notes: This is Simplified Chinese (used in mainland China, Singapore). See zh-TW.json for Traditional Chinese (Taiwan, Hong Kong).
